data.title: ICE Review: Weaker Chicago Soy Weighs on Canola
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Intercontinental Exchange canola futures saw another round of losses on Friday but a broker noted the Canadian oilseed remained cheaper when compared to product values.

The Chicago soy complex was weaker, especially with sharp declines in soyoil. European rapeseed was down as well, but there were gains in Malaysian palm oil. Losses in crude oil were spilling over to further weaken the vegetable oils.

The broker added the trade is getting fed up with the flip-flops over United States tariffs. Although President Donald Trump stated he will impose his levies against imports from Canada and Mexico on Tuesday, he also opened the door to an extension to April.

Also, the broker said ongoing uncertainty over U.S. biofuel tax credits needs to be resolved. In January, changes to the credits removed canola's eligibility and the Trump administration has yet to indicate their intentions towards the credits.

Cumulative figures from the Canadian Grain Commission showed canola exports of 6.02 million tonnes are almost double those a year ago.

The Canadian dollar was lower Friday afternoon with the loonie pulling back to 69.17 U.S. cents compared to Thursday's close of 69.34.

Today marked the first notice day for March futures.

There were 37,630 contracts traded on Friday, compared to 40,936 on Thursday. Spreading accounted for 19,558 contracts traded.

data.title: Canada's Foreign Minister Says New Economic, Security Partners Needed Amid Shift in U.S.
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canadian Foreign Minister Melanie Joly said her country needs deeper economic and security ties with Europe and parts of Asia amid the geopolitical shift unfolding under President Trump.

"We have not necessarily had the reflex to diversify trade, and we've been overreliant on the U.S.," Joly said Friday at a business event in Vancouver, British Columbia. "We need new alliances and new partnerships. We need to diversify, not only the trade side, but also on the security front."

Trump said this week he's on course to slap tariffs of up to 25% on imports from Canada starting March 4. Nearly 20% of Canada's gross domestic product is tied to exports to the U.S., and Bank of Canada officials have warned a prolonged trade conflict between Ottawa and Washington would have "severe" repercussions for the economy.

"When they negotiate with us, they don't only talk about trade, they actually talk about a series of different topics," Joly said of talks to avert tariffs on the country's U.S.-bound exports. She added Canadian officials have repeatedly warned U.S. officials and lawmakers that hefty tariffs on Canadian imports will result in higher prices for U.S. consumers and potential job losses, given the integration between the two economies.

Since Trump's tariff threats, Canadian politicians and business leaders have talked about the need to expand business opportunities with non-U.S. markets. Export Development Canada, the country's export-credit agency, told The Wall Street Journal this week it has witnessed a surge in interest from companies looking to explore new markets as they brace for tariffs.

"We're having conversations now with countries that we are not that close to," Joly told the Vancouver audience. "We're saying, 'We need to share intelligence. We need to do more defense cooperation. We need more of your trade, and we need also more of your knowledge.'"

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Inflation didn't get any worse. When will it start going down again?
data.description:
By Jeffry Bartash

Rising prices are still a challenge for the Fed

Inflation is still a sore spot for the U.S. economy, but at least it didn't get much worse at the start of the year.

That's the best that can be said about the latest reading of the personal consumption expenditures price index, the Federal Reserve's preferred tool to track the inflation rate.

The PCE index rose a sharper-than-desired 0.3% in January, but it came in a few ticks cooler than the better-known consumer-price index.

Read: U.S. inflation rate doesn't get any worse, PCE shows, but latest rise to keep Fed sidelined

What's more, the 12-month rate of inflation slowed to 2.5% from 2.6%.

The PCE index is considered the most accurate measure of U.S. inflation. The Fed is aiming to bring down the rate of inflation to 2%, returning it close to low prepandemic levels.

The Fed isn't far off the mark, but its effort to tame inflation has suffered a setback since late last year.

The rate of inflation, in fact, had dropped to as low as 2.1% in September before heading higher again.

"Inflation is back under the Fed's microscope and is more stubborn than policy makers had hoped," said senior economist Ben Ayers of Nationwide.

Why is it so stubborn?

Blame a strong U.S. economy, for one thing: Strong demand for goods and service pushes up the price of supplies and labor.

Some inflation also appears to have gotten embedded in the economy, making it harder to eradicate.

Whatever the case, the rebound in inflation has forced the Fed to put further reductions in U.S. interest rates on ice. And it means that lending costs are likely to remain at punishing levels for home buyers and other borrowers.

The future path of inflation, meanwhile, has gotten murkier.

One of the chief threats is the potential that major tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump could raise the cost of many imported goods. Fed officials are watching closely to try to figure out how tariffs could affect prices.

"Higher tariffs will raise prices of imported goods as well as made-in-America manufacturers, whose producers can charge higher prices with less foreign competition," said chief economist Bill Adams of Comerica Bank in Dallas.

For now, the threat of tariffs is mostly that. The White House has hit China with new charges, but it has held off on other major moves for now.

Also read: Consumer spending posts first drop in 22 months, but it's probably not a bad omen for the economy

The more immediate focus will shift to the U.S. employment report for February, which comes out next Friday.

Wall Street DJIA SPX hopes to see slower growth in wages and mild employment growth, both of which would suggest that labor costs are not adding much to inflation.

-Jeffry Bartash

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Ameresco, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Ameresco, Inc., a leading provider of energy efficiency and renewable energy solutions, has released its 2024 10-K report, detailing significant financial growth, strategic initiatives, and the challenges faced over the past year. The report highlights the company's commitment to expanding its energy solutions portfolio and addressing the global demand for sustainable energy.

Financial Highlights

Ameresco, Inc. reported robust financial performance for the year 2024, marked by substantial growth in key metrics:

  • Revenues: $1,769.9 million, reflecting a 28.8% increase from the previous year, primarily due to a significant rise in project revenue.
  • Gross Profit: $256.1 million, representing a slight increase of 3.9% from the prior year, with a gross profit margin of 14.5% impacted by cost overruns on large-scale projects.
  • Operating Income: $108.7 million, a 32.3% increase year-over-year, driven by a gain on the sale of a business and higher project revenues.
  • Net Income: $53.9 million, a decrease of 15.6% compared to the previous year, influenced by higher interest expenses and asset impairments.
  • Net Income Per Share: Basic EPS was $1.08, and Diluted EPS was $1.07, both reflecting a decrease from the previous year due to the factors affecting net income.

Business Highlights

Ameresco's business performance in 2024 showcased significant operational achievements and strategic expansions:

  • Revenue Segments: The company's business is organized into several segments, including North America Regions, U.S. Federal, Renewable Fuels, Europe, and All Other. North America Regions contributed the largest portion of revenue.
  • Geographical Performance: The North America Regions segment saw a significant increase in project revenues, while the Europe segment experienced a notable rise in project revenue due to active projects in the United Kingdom. The U.S. Federal segment experienced a decrease in project revenue due to the timing of revenue recognition.
  • Sales Units: As of December 31, 2024, Ameresco owned and operated 209 small-scale renewable energy plants, including solar PV installations, with a combined capacity of approximately 731 megawatt equivalents. The company also has energy assets in development and construction with a combined capacity of approximately 637 MWe.
  • Future Outlook: Ameresco anticipates continued demand for its energy solutions driven by the global emphasis on climate change and reducing carbon emissions. The company plans to invest significantly in capital projects and expand its service offerings through strategic acquisitions and joint ventures.

Strategic Initiatives

Ameresco has undertaken several strategic initiatives to bolster its market position and drive long-term growth:

  • Acquisitions and Divestitures: The company expanded its energy solutions portfolio through strategic acquisitions, such as the acquisition of BCE, which includes interests in solar, battery, and microgrid assets. Ameresco also divested an energy technology and advisory services company, generating a net gain of $38 million.
  • Capital Investments: Ameresco invested $417 million in new energy assets during 2024, focusing on the development of small-scale renewable energy plants.
  • Capital Management: The company refinanced its Senior Secured Credit Facility to provide a $225 million revolving credit facility and a $100 million term loan, both maturing in December 2028. Additionally, Ameresco entered into a second lien credit agreement for a $100 million term loan maturing in June 2029. The company also utilized sale-leaseback arrangements to finance energy assets, with $399.4 million classified as long-term financing facilities.
  • Future Outlook: Looking ahead, Ameresco plans to invest approximately $350 million to $400 million in capital investments in 2025, primarily for the construction or acquisition of new renewable energy plants. The company also anticipates additional financings of $300 million to $350 million in 2025 to support these initiatives.

Challenges and Risks

Ameresco faces several significant risks and challenges that could impact its business operations and financial performance:

  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to fluctuations in demand for energy efficiency and renewable energy solutions. If the market does not grow as expected, revenues may suffer.
  • Operational Risks: The extended sales cycle for projects, supply chain disruptions, and reliance on third-party subcontractors and suppliers pose risks to project completion and profitability. Additionally, the company faces challenges related to the availability and cost of raw materials, such as lithium for battery storage.
  • Regulatory Risks: Changes in government policies and regulations, particularly those affecting energy efficiency and renewable energy incentives, could adversely impact the company's operations. The potential review and termination of funds appropriated through the IRA and IIJA could delay or cancel projects.
  • Financial Risks: The company is subject to credit risks from customers, particularly government entities, which could impact cash flow and financial performance. The company's indebtedness and associated covenants may limit operational flexibility.
  • Environmental and Climate Risks: Extreme weather events and natural disasters, exacerbated by climate change, could disrupt operations and increase costs.
  • Cybersecurity Risks: The company is vulnerable to cyberattacks and data breaches, which could harm its reputation and operations.

Management is focused on mitigating these risks through strategic planning and operational adjustments, enhancing supply chain resilience, exploring alternative financing options, and investing in cybersecurity measures to protect its IT infrastructure.

SEC Filing: Ameresco, Inc. [ AMRC ] - 10-K - Feb. 28, 2025

data.title: Stellantis Names New Head of North America Communications
data.description:
Stellantis Names New Head of North America Communications

PR Newswire

AUBURN HILLS, Mich., Feb. 28, 2025

AUBURN HILLS, Mich., Feb. 28, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Stellantis today announced the appointment of Kaileen Connelly to Senior Vice President of North America Communications, effective March 10. In her new role, Connelly will oversee the Company's regional communications strategy.

Connelly has been an integral part of Stellantis since its inception in early 2021, leading the global corporate communications content team and supporting top executives in shaping and promoting the company's narrative. Before joining Stellantis, she managed key automotive, business and technology accounts at renowned marketing and public relations firms, including Weber Shandwick and MullenLowe.

In a related move, after 26-plus years with the company, Shawn Morgan has announced her intention to retire.

"This is a pinnacle year for us in North America and Kaileen's expertise in crafting compelling narratives and driving strategic communications will be invaluable as we write our next chapter," said Antonio Filosa, Chief Operating Officer for the Americas and Chief Quality Officer. "I want to thank Shawn for her unwavering dedication, passion and leadership. She's been an invaluable part of our journey and I wish her and her family well."

Connelly holds a Master of Science in Mass Communication from Boston University and a Bachelor of Arts from Wayne State University in Detroit, Michigan.

Stellantis North America

Stellantis is one of the world's leading automakers, aiming to provide clean, safe and affordable freedom of mobility for all. In North America, it's best known for producing and selling vehicles in a portfolio of iconic, innovative and award-winning brands, including Jeep(R), Chrysler, Dodge//SRT, Ram, Alfa Romeo and Fiat. Stellantis is executing its Dare Forward 2030, a bold strategic plan that paves the way to achieve the ambitious target of becoming a carbon net zero mobility tech company by 2038, while creating added value for all stakeholders.

data.title: Stanford University to Sell $327 Million of Taxable Bonds
data.description:
By Chris Wack

Stanford University is offering $327 million of taxable bonds, and plans to use proceeds from the sale for general purposes at the university.

The university is selling Series 2025 A taxable bonds, which will be due March 1, 2035, according to documents posted Thursday on MuniOS.

Interest payments will be made semiannually on March 1 and Sept. 1, with the first payment due Sept. 1, 2025.

The bonds are unsecured general obligations of the university, and aren't secured by a reserve fund.

Stanford is a research and teaching university, with both graduate and undergraduate programs. For the fall 2024 semester, the university had 7,550 undergraduate and 9,900 graduate students. Its main campus consists of 8,400 acres of land near Palo Alto, Calif.

Sources of revenue include student tuition and fees, and revenue from healthcare services, sponsored support and investment income, according to the preliminary official statement.

Moody's rated the securities Aaa, and S&P Global Ratings and Fitch Ratings have them rated at AAA.

Morgan Stanley is the lead manager on the deal.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: Market Chatter: Alphabet's Google to Cut Staff in HR, Cloud Division
data.description:
Alphabet's Google plans to cut staff in both its human resources and cloud divisions as part of internal restructuring, CNBC reported Thursday, citing company memos.

The company will offer a voluntary exit program for full-time HR employees based in the US starting in early March, the report said, citing a memo issued Tuesday by HR chief Fiona Cicconi.

In the cloud unit, the company made cuts to several teams, mostly affecting operations support staff, with some roles moving to India and Mexico, the report said.

Google didn't immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Federal Budget Cuts Seen as Potential Risk for Munis — Market Talk
data.description: Federal tax discussions pose potential threats to municipal bonds, PGIM Fixed Income's Jason Appleson says. As Congress looks to scale down spending to offset tax cuts. data.title: Corn Futures Slide as Traders End Month With Tariff Uncertainty - Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Corn for May delivery fell 2.4%, to $4.69 1/2 a bushel, on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, closing the last day of trading in February lower on this week's tariff threats from President Trump.
  • Wheat for May delivery fell 1.2%, to $5.55 3/4 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for May delivery fell 1.2%, to $10.25 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Growing Fears: CBOT grains were under pressure, with traders growing increasingly worried about a greater likelihood of a recession. This is because of recent weaker economic data and rising uncertainty around Trump's next tariff actions, with 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico now expected next week. "Tariffs are normally not bullish on commodities, that the U.S. overproduces and exports in abundance," said Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note. "The odds are growing for U.S. recession as debt levels soar and demand softens."

Tethered: Corn led the CBOT down, and wheat took its cues from corn. "Combo of bearish U.S. corn and wheat Ag Outlook balance sheet projections, better weather in the U.S. and South America, and end of month tech selling seems to be hitting corn and wheat in particular this week," said Joel Karlin of Ocean State Research. The heated exchange between Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky also attracted attention from traders, weighing on futures late in the session.

INSIGHT

Weak Week: Commodity futures across the board lost ground this week, according to data compiled by Saxo Bank. The firm said that all commodities, with the exception of live cattle, fell for the week. Sugar futures were the biggest loser, falling nearly 7%. Other leading decliners were wheat and natural gas. The volatile flow of news out of Washington is a main factor pressuring commodities, said Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank. "Tariff fatigue emerged this week as global financial markets scrambled to keep up with the barrage of executive orders and off the cuff remarks from President Trump," he said.

Across the Pond: Palm oil--an indicator monitored by U.S. traders to compare with soybean oil--ended higher amid tight supply. Despite recent supply concerns, palm-oil inventory at key producing countries is likely to reach a balance in March and April, and may start to accumulate after May if palm oil remains at high prices, Minmetals Futures said in a research note. Global consumption of vegetable oils in 2025 also remained uncertain after Malaysia and Brazil delayed raising their mandatory biodiesel blend rates.

Finding a New Use: The National Oilseed Processors Association is advocating for the Energy Department to include oilseed-based biofuels like biodiesel and renewable diesel in its definition of sustainable maritime fuel--similar to SAF, or sustainable aviation fuel. NOPA says that "the maritime shipping sector is a key stakeholder in the transition to sustainable fuels and presents a significant opportunity for emissions reductions." Renewable fuels has been seen as a key sector for growing vegetable-oil-refining capacity in the U.S.

AHEAD

  • The USDA will release its weekly Grain Export Inspections report at 11 a.m. ET Monday.
  • The USDA will release its monthly Grain Crushings report at 3 p.m. ET Monday.
  • The EIA will release its weekly ethanol production and stocks report at 10:30 a.m. ET Wednesday.
  • Sherry Qin contributed to this article.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Oil States' Debt Seen Safer Under Trump, Manufacturing Riskier — Market Talk
data.description:
1508 ET - Local governments and institutions in the crosshairs of the new administration could lose federal funding, potentially limiting their capacity to pay out their bonds, PGIM Fixed Income's Jason Appleson says. Conversely, risks could be shrinking for agencies more aligned with the new trends in Washington, or more immune to tariffs and deportations, for example. "As the oil industry opens the floodgates, Texas, Alaska, Oklahoma could benefit," he says. On the contrary, states relying on manufacturing could suffer if trade wars make imported inputs more expensive while possibly curbing exports. "It will be very state specific," Appleson says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1505 ET - Shifting federal policies push PGIM Fixed Income's Jason Appleson, head of municipal bonds, to re-evaluate holdings in light of President Trump's agenda. The firm has $20 billion under management across six municipal-bond funds. Appleson says new policies could impact the credit worthiness of states, towns, hospitals, universities and other issuers. He is selling bonds issued by entities he now believes are more likely to suffer from federal policies and replacing them by ones with less perceived risk. "We are looking at things that look riskier now than when we bought them," he says. "Municipal bonds have a robust secondary market that allows an active manager to replace risk." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1407 ET - New figures indicate Canadian energy production hit record levels in December. The report, issued by Statistics Canada, was overlooked due to the release of surprisingly strong 4Q GDP data. The data agency says crude-oil production in Canada rose 2% in December from a year ago to 27.1 million cubic meters, or a record. Exports of crude oil and equivalents also reached a record in December, up 2.1% from a year ago, driven primarily by shipments to the US via rail and tanker ship. Natural-gas output also reached a record. President Trump is threatening to impose a 10% tariff on Canadian imports of energy products starting March 4. Data indicate that 95% of Canada's crude-oil exports were US-bound. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1312 ET - Jefferies pounds the table again today on Nike, with analyst Randal Konik on a call with investors saying he's "massively bullish" on the stock. Jefferies earlier this week upgraded Nike to buy from hold and upped the price target to $115 from $75. Konik says he was very negative on Nike over the past two years because of market share losses and categories slowing that put earnings at risk. Konik says Nike's former CEO John Donahoe was the wrong leader for the sportswear brand given his background at eBay. Meanwhile, current CEO Elliott Hill knows that Nike has to improve product and balance distribution, Konik says. "If Walmart can double in 2 years, why can't Nike?," Konik adds. Nike shares are down 2% to $78.50. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1243 ET - Bank of Nova Scotia economists Jean-François Perrault and Rebekah Young argue candidates vying to win this year's Canadian election and be prime minister should commit to raise the country's per-capita GDP. They say the Liberal and Conservative leaders should pledge to raise Canada's per-capita GDP by 2% a year on a sustained basis. Per-capita GDP--also a gauge of living standards--fell 1.4% in 2024 and 1.3% in 2023. Scotiabank acknowledges this effort requires "staggering" work, and an estimated increase in annual capital spending of about C$60 billion. But targeting annual per-capita GDP growth of 2% would result in an annual income gain of C$1,200 per person. "Canadians should demand better results from their governments," Scotiabank says. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1239 ET - HP Inc. sees its recent acquisition of Humane's AI-powered platform helping to create a more seamless user experience. CEO Enrique Lores says one example will be to help PCs and video conferencing rooms connect more seamlessly "which we know is a big pain point for customers," he says on the earnings call. "What you will see us doing is use those total assets to accelerate the deployment of AI at the edge and accelerate our plans there, and also to accelerate what we call the better-together experience," Lores adds. The computer and printer maker made a deal for certain AI assets from Humane for $116 million earlier in the month. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1215 ET - HP Inc. joins the chorus of other companies touting its changing footprint. The computer and printer maker says it expects more than 90% of its products sold in North America to be built outside of China by the end of fiscal 2025. CEO Enrique Lores on an earnings call says China will continue to be an important manufacturing hub for the rest of the world. He adds that HP is managing tariff increases on China and has included them in its outlook, while also being ready to manage any changes that come with additional tariffs. "Depending on the scope, while some of our mitigating actions can take a few months lead there we would be focused on fully offsetting over time," says Lores. Shares retreat 7.2% to $30.74. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

By Matt Grossman

A closely watched real-time GDP estimate published by the Atlanta Fed now indicates the economy might be on track to contract in the three months through March.

After downbeat economic data released Friday, the GDPNow tracker suggests first-quarter growth is running at a minus 1.5% annualized pace.

If the figures hold, it would be the first time that GDP has contracted since the first three months of 2022.

The GDPNow tracker incorporates data as they are released to build a picture of how the economy is faring. Before Friday, GDPNow was estimating 2.3% growth this quarter. The first official estimate of first-quarter GDP growth is due from the Commerce Department on April 30, a month after the quarter ends.

Two data points released on Friday were responsible for the decline.

A preliminary estimate indicated a surge in the U.S. trade deficit in goods in January. Imports that exceed exports subtract from GDP.

The Commerce Department released a report that showed personal consumption expenditures decreased by 0.2% in January. Consumer spending is still expected to be a positive contribution to GDP growth, but less so than the GDPNow model previously believed.

After the data were released, Goldman Sachs lowered its first-quarter GDP-growth estimate. The investment bank now thinks growth will be at a 1.4% annualized pace in the three-month period, down from 1.8% previously.

Piper Sandler economists said in a note to clients they think that GDP may shrink on a quarter-over-quarter basis in the three months through March, in a report warning that stagflation is "now a real risk."

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

1200 ET - Economists at the Atlanta Fed study Trump administration tariff proposals and estimate that they could push up the prices of everyday retail items by up to 1.6%. The study suggests a basket of items like food, beverages and general merchandise might increase by between 0.81% to 1.63%, depending on how much of the cost increases companies pass along. The effect on overall consumer inflation would be different, because everyday retail items are only a portion of the CPI basket, but other CPI categories may experience residual price pressures. The work by economists Salome Baslandze, Simon Fuchs, KC Pringle and Michael Dwight Sparks assumes an additional 10% tariff on China, 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico, and a 10% tariff on other countries. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1153 ET - Praxis Precision Medicines is still a buy despite the late-stage study setback for ulixacaltamide in essential tremor, analysts at Truist say. In a research note, Truist says it still likes Praxis for vormatrigine in focal onset seizures and relutrigine in developmental and epileptic epilepsies, which the firm says puts a floor on the stock. Truist removes essential tremor from its Praxis valuation, which drops its price target on the stock to $85 from $175. Praxis down 40% to $39.26. (colin.kellaher@wsj.com)

1111 ET - Canada's 4Q GDP report is positive but unfortunately will go down as a "relic" due to the pending fallout from President Trump's trade policy, says economist David Rosenberg, head of Rosenberg Research. The president says the 25% levy on nonenergy goods from Canada, and a 10% tariff on U.S.-bound energy products, is on course for March 4. "If the Trump tariffs go through as advertised, today's [GDP] number will soon be long forgotten," Rosenberg says. Were it not for the drawdown in inventories, Rosenberg estimates Canada's economy would have jumped 6% annualized in 4Q, versus the actual 2.6% print. Rosenberg says aside from tariffs, there is likely a slowdown ahead in consumer spending given the government's sales-tax holiday ended in mid-February. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

Rising supply and falling demand--housing inventory hit a 2020 peak while pending sales dropped to a record low, excluding early in the pandemic--favor buyers, some of whom cancel during inspections for better options. Economic uncertainty, including tariffs and layoffs, plus high mortgage rates--6.96% in January--and a 4.1% rise in median home prices, fueled buyer hesitation. Cancellation rates exceed typical early-year levels but fall short of peaks like 16.4% in March 2020 or late 2022, when mortgage rates soared above 7% for the first time in 20 years. Redfin says buyers should monitor desired homes, as deals collapse post-bidding wars. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1508 ET - Local governments and institutions in the crosshairs of the new administration could lose federal funding, potentially limiting their capacity to pay out their bonds, PGIM Fixed Income's Jason Appleson says. Conversely, risks could be shrinking for agencies more aligned with the new trends in Washington, or more immune to tariffs and deportations, for example. "As the oil industry opens the floodgates, Texas, Alaska, Oklahoma could benefit," he says. On the contrary, states relying on manufacturing could suffer if trade wars make imported inputs more expensive while possibly curbing exports. "It will be very state specific," Appleson says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1505 ET - Shifting federal policies push PGIM Fixed Income's Jason Appleson, head of municipal bonds, to re-evaluate holdings in light of President Trump's agenda. The firm has $20 billion under management across six municipal-bond funds. Appleson says new policies could impact the credit worthiness of states, towns, hospitals, universities and other issuers. He is selling bonds issued by entities he now believes are more likely to suffer from federal policies and replacing them by ones with less perceived risk. "We are looking at things that look riskier now than when we bought them," he says. "Municipal bonds have a robust secondary market that allows an active manager to replace risk." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1243 ET - Bank of Nova Scotia economists Jean-François Perrault and Rebekah Young argue candidates vying to win this year's Canadian election and be prime minister should commit to raise the country's per-capita GDP. They say the Liberal and Conservative leaders should pledge to raise Canada's per-capita GDP by 2% a year on a sustained basis. Per-capita GDP--also a gauge of living standards--fell 1.4% in 2024 and 1.3% in 2023. Scotiabank acknowledges this effort requires "staggering" work, and an estimated increase in annual capital spending of about C$60 billion. But targeting annual per-capita GDP growth of 2% would result in an annual income gain of C$1,200 per person. "Canadians should demand better results from their governments," Scotiabank says. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1217 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.6% at $9,357 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 1% at $2,604 a ton. Base metals have been pressured lower as the U.S. dollar gained on renewed tariff concerns. President Trump's administration has initiated a Section 232 copper investigation to address worries over U.S. import dependency, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a note. This is expected to result in import tariffs on copper in all forms and export controls on copper scrap and concentrate to China, and will likely be implemented within 9 months, Goldman says. A 25% tariff is the most likely outcome and will fully pass through into U.S. domestic copper prices, Goldman writes. The effect on LME copper prices will likely be limited, as trade flows adjust, rather than disrupt global supply, analysts add. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

By Matt Grossman

A closely watched real-time GDP estimate published by the Atlanta Fed now indicates the economy might be on track to contract in the three months through March.

After downbeat economic data released Friday, the GDPNow tracker suggests first-quarter growth is running at a minus 1.5% annualized pace.

If the figures hold, it would be the first time that GDP has contracted since the first three months of 2022.

The GDPNow tracker incorporates data as they are released to build a picture of how the economy is faring. Before Friday, GDPNow was estimating 2.3% growth this quarter. The first official estimate of first-quarter GDP growth is due from the Commerce Department on April 30, a month after the quarter ends.

Two data points released on Friday were responsible for the decline.

A preliminary estimate indicated a surge in the U.S. trade deficit in goods in January. Imports that exceed exports subtract from GDP.

The Commerce Department released a report that showed personal consumption expenditures decreased by 0.2% in January. Consumer spending is still expected to be a positive contribution to GDP growth, but less so than the GDPNow model previously believed.

After the data were released, Goldman Sachs lowered its first-quarter GDP-growth estimate. The investment bank now thinks growth will be at a 1.4% annualized pace in the three-month period, down from 1.8% previously.

Piper Sandler economists said in a note to clients they think that GDP may shrink on a quarter-over-quarter basis in the three months through March, in a report warning that stagflation is "now a real risk."

Write to Matt Grossman at matt.grossman@wsj.com

1206 ET - Commodity futures across the board lost ground this week, according to data compiled by Saxo Bank. The firm says that all commodities, with the exception of live cattle, fell for the week. Sugar futures were the biggest loser, falling nearly 7% for the week. Other leading declinerswere wheat and natural gas. The volatile flow of news out of Washington is a main factor pressuring commodities, says Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank. "Tariff fatigue emerged this week as global financial markets scrambled to keep up with the barrage of executive orders and off the cuff remarks from President Trump," he says. Even gold couldn't mount a positive week, this while the U.S. dollar is growing stronger. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

The work by economists Salome Baslandze, Simon Fuchs, KC Pringle and Michael Dwight Sparks assumes an additional 10% tariff on China, 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico, and a 10% tariff on other countries. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1111 ET - Canada's 4Q GDP report is positive but unfortunately will go down as a "relic" due to the pending fallout from President Trump's trade policy, says economist David Rosenberg, head of Rosenberg Research. The president says the 25% levy on nonenergy goods from Canada, and a 10% tariff on U.S.-bound energy products, is on course for March 4. "If the Trump tariffs go through as advertised, today's [GDP] number will soon be long forgotten," Rosenberg says. Were it not for the drawdown in inventories, Rosenberg estimates Canada's economy would have jumped 6% annualized in 4Q, versus the actual 2.6% print. Rosenberg says aside from tariffs, there is likely a slowdown ahead in consumer spending given the government's sales-tax holiday ended in mid-February. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1011 ET - In January, just over 41,000 U.S. home-purchase deals, or 14.3% of contracts, fell through, the highest rate for this time of year since 2017 and up from 13.4% last year, Redfin says. Rising supply and falling demand--housing inventory hit a 2020 peak while pending sales dropped to a record low, excluding early in the pandemic--favor buyers, some of whom cancel during inspections for better options. Economic uncertainty, including tariffs and layoffs, plus high mortgage rates--6.96% in January--and a 4.1% rise in median home prices, fueled buyer hesitation. Cancellation rates exceed typical early-year levels but fall short of peaks like 16.4% in March 2020 or late 2022, when mortgage rates soared above 7% for the first time in 20 years. Redfin says buyers should monitor desired homes, as deals collapse post-bidding wars. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1001 ET - U.S. consumers are showing signs of price fatigue, Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan and Shannon Grein write. January PCE inflation cooled a little, to 2.5% from December's 2.6%, inching closer to the Fed's 2% target. Quinlan and Grein note that "inflation-adjusted spending dropped half a percentage point, the biggest monthly retrenchment in almost four years." PCE data show consumers trimming spending on cars and recreational goods. Quinlan and Grein say that the inflation outlook is uncertain, though. "Consumers are price fatigued, and major retailers have acknowledged this," while tariffs threaten to keep upward pressure on prices, they write. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0955 ET - Aggressive Bank of Canada rate cuts fuel stronger-than-expected growth in the country in the second half of 2024, says Benjamin Reitzes, economist at BMO Capital Markets. In 4Q, household spending rose 5.6% annualized in 4Q, GDP data indicate. Reitzes says household outlays rose 3.6% or better in three of the four quarters in 2024. A sales-tax holiday may have helped 4Q data, but that measure only kicked in on December or late in 4Q, indicating momentum "was firm" beforehand, Reitzes says. The GDP data indicate the household savings rate fell in 4Q, to 6.1% from 7.3% in the prior quarter, although Statistics Canada notes that remains higher than the 3.7% level for 2023. After-tax income in Canada climbed nearly 8% in 4Q from a year ago. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira) 0954 ET - For bond investors, the fact that inflation remains above central bank targets in some economies will be a cause for concern, says Chris Iggo at AXA Investment Managers in a note. While nominal yields in German Bunds barely exceed the current German inflation rate, elsewhere there is a positive gap, the CIO for AXA IM Core says. "But, worryingly, in some cases that gap has been closing. In the U.K. gilt market, the gap between the 5-year yield and the annual inflation rate fell to 120 basis points in January from 200 basis points last September, he says. Also the recent rally in U.S. Treasurys has reduced the gap in the U.S. government bond market as well. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1508 ET - Local governments and institutions in the crosshairs of the new administration could lose federal funding, potentially limiting their capacity to pay out their bonds, PGIM Fixed Income's Jason Appleson says. Conversely, risks could be shrinking for agencies more aligned with the new trends in Washington, or more immune to tariffs and deportations, for example. 1407 ET - New figures indicate Canadian energy production hit record levels in December. The report, issued by Statistics Canada, was overlooked due to the release of surprisingly strong 4Q GDP data. The data agency says crude-oil production in Canada rose 2% in December from a year ago to 27.1 million cubic meters, or a record. Exports of crude oil and equivalents also reached a record in December, up 2.1% from a year ago, driven primarily by shipments to the US via rail and tanker ship. Natural-gas output also reached a record. President Trump is threatening to impose a 10% tariff on Canadian imports of energy products starting March 4. Data indicate that 95% of Canada's crude-oil exports were US-bound. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1328 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. fell by two this week to 486, a first decline in five weeks, and was down by 20 from a year ago, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. Rigs drilling for natural gas increased by three to 102, or 17 fewer than a year ago. Top U.S. natural gas producer Expand Energy said this week it expects to run about 12 rigs in 2025 with average production estimated at 7.1 billion cubic feet equivalent a day. The company plans to add capacity in the second half, running around 15 rigs, and end the year at 7.2 Bcfe/d, raising that to an average of 7.5 Bcfe/d in 2026 "should market conditions warrant." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1206 ET - Commodity futures across the board lost ground this week, according to data compiled by Saxo Bank. The firm says that all commodities, with the exception of live cattle, fell for the week. Sugar futures were the biggest loser, falling nearly 7% for the week. Other leading declinerswere wheat and natural gas. The volatile flow of news out of Washington is a main factor pressuring commodities, says Ole Hansen of Saxo Bank. "Tariff fatigue emerged this week as global financial markets scrambled to keep up with the barrage of executive orders and off the cuff remarks from President Trump," he says. Even gold couldn't mount a positive week, this while the U.S. dollar is growing stronger. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1148 ET - The National Oilseed Processors Association is advocating for the Department of Energy to include oliseed-based biofuels like biodiesel and renewable diesel in its definition of sustainable maritime fuel — similar to SAF, or sustainable aviation fuel. NOPA says that "the maritime shipping sector is a key stakeholder in the transition to sustainable fuels and presents a significant opportunity for emissions reductions." Renewable fuels has been seen as a key sector for growing vegetable-oil refining capacity in the U.S. — particularly for soybean oil. Most-active soybeans are down 1% in late-morning trade, while corn sinks 2.2% and wheat falls 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1054 ET - EU gas storage continues to deplete rapidly, with current withdrawal rates indicating significantly higher refilling needs for next winter, says Commerzbank Research's Carsten Fritsch. According to industry group Gas Infrastructure Europe, inventories are now 39.5% full. At the current rate, the filling level would fall to just over 30% in a month and EU gas stocks drop to around 35 billion cubic meters by the end of March. "This would mean a refilling requirement of around 70 billion cubic meters in order to completely replenish the storage facilities for the next winter," the commodity analyst says. In contrast, only 40 billion cubic meters were required last year. The benchmark TTF gas contract currently trades 1.5% lower at 44.46 euros a barrel amid milder temperatures, talks to relax EU refilling targets and prospects that the war in Ukraine could soon end. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1003 ET - Crude's gains on the U.S. decision to revoke a license under which Chevron produces oil in Venezuela prove fleeting, with futures back in the red as the market awaits more clarity on U.S. import tariffs and whether or not OPEC+ will again extend the timeline for returning barrels to the market. "Oil prices are fluctuating within a trading range, but a delay will give prices an upside breakout," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. WTI is off 1.5% and trading back below $70 a barrel, and Brent is down 1.4% at $73.02 with the April contract expiring at the close. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0920 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are holding below the $4 mark with March weather continuing to look warmer than previously expected. "With the bearish weather forecast over the last week indicating storage deficits liable to peak near a manageable 250 Bcf, and daily production rebounding to test early-February highs, near-term downside is favored," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. The inventory deficit over the five-year average widened last week to 238 Bcf from 118 Bcf the week before. "The cold winter has accelerated the bullish narrative while prompt storage deficits provide fundamental cover for longs," Rubin adds. The Nymex front month is off 1.3% at $3.884/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0827 ET - Eni remains committed to energy transition-related investments as its oil and gas peers withdraw from them, HSBC analysts write. The Italian oil major's 2025 to 28 strategy doubles down on biorefining and renewable assets, they write. It's new strategy contained no major changes, they write. This is somewhat remarkable given peers BP, Equinor and Shell have all recently scaled back their climate ambitions and investments in low-carbon assets, they write. Shares trade down 1.6% at 13.85 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0815 ET - Eni's share buyback could grow to 2.5 billion euros this year, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo and Kai Ye Loh write. The Italian energy major plans to initially buy back 1.5 billion euros of shares over the year but said it could be as high as 3.5 billion euros. In reality, it is more likely to be between 1.8 billion and 2.5 billion euros, the analysts write. Overall, the oil major's revised strategy contains higher-than-expected distributions, higher upstream production and unchanged capital expenditure guidance despite exchange-rate headwinds, the analysts write. Shares trade down 1.6% at 13.85 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0751 ET - Eni's updated strategy contained no surprises and effectively extends prior strategic guidance, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. The strategy, set out Thursday, continues to focus on value creation in its upstream division and a focused energy transition strategy which is already working, they write. The Italian energy major has continued its joint-venture 'satellite' model but as this proliferates, investors may begin to wonder whether the benefits are offset by the complicated nature of its portfolio, they add. "Understanding cash flow trends over time will depend on the dividend policies of such entities, and this creates less transparency," they write. Shares trade down 1.6% at 13.85 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0514 ET - Palm oil ended higher amid tight supply. Despite recent supply concerns, palm oil inventory at key producing countries is likely to reach a balance in March and April, and may start to accumulate after May if palm oil remains at high prices, Minmetals Futures said in a research note. Global consumption of vegetable oils in 2025 also remained uncertain after Malaysia and Brazil delayed raising their mandatory biodiesel blend rates. Meanwhile, cargo surveyor AmSpec Agri Malaysia estimated that Malaysia's palm oil exports during the Feb. 1-28 period dropped 8.51% on month. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery rose 42 ringgit at 4,553 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: TOOTSIE ROLL INDUSTRIES INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Tootsie Roll Industries Inc., a leading confectionery manufacturer known for its iconic brands such as TOOTSIE ROLL, TOOTSIE POPS, CHARMS, DOTS, and JUNIOR MINTS, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the competitive confectionery market.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $723.217 million, reflecting a decrease from the previous year due to lower sales volumes.
  • Product Gross Margin: $247.474 million, benefiting from cost reductions and higher price realizations.
  • Earnings from Operations: $100.505 million, slightly down from the previous year, primarily due to decreased net product sales.
  • Net Earnings: $86.808 million, impacted by a non-cash tax charge related to deferred tax assets.
  • Net Earnings Attributable to Tootsie Roll Industries, Inc. Per Share: $1.22, a decrease of $0.06 per share or 4.7% from the previous year.

Business Highlights

  • Product Lines and Trademarks: Tootsie Roll Industries operates exclusively in the confectionery segment, with products sold under well-known trademarks such as TOOTSIE ROLL, TOOTSIE POPS, CHARMS, DOTS, and JUNIOR MINTS. These products are marketed in various packages suitable for different retail outlets.
  • Geographical Performance: The company's principal markets are the United States, Canada, and Mexico. Most production from Canadian plants is sold in the U.S., while the majority of production from the Mexican plant is sold within Mexico.
  • Competitive Landscape: The domestic confectionery market is highly competitive, with Tootsie Roll Industries competing primarily on brand recognition, retail shelf space, and pricing. The company is among the ten largest domestic manufacturers in this industry.
  • Seasonal Workforce: The company employs approximately 2,300 full-time employees, with additional seasonal workers hired to meet increased production demands, particularly for the Halloween season.
  • Union Agreements: The company has a union labor agreement at its Chicago plant, effective until September 2027, and a contract with its Canadian plant's labor union, effective until January 2029.
  • Customer Concentration: Significant sales are made to Wal-Mart and Dollar Tree, with these customers accounting for a substantial portion of net product sales. The loss of one or more significant customers could materially affect the business.
  • Operational Adjustments: In response to rising input costs, the company plans to increase sales prices and may adjust product sizes and weights. The company also engages in hedging to manage commodity cost fluctuations.
  • Future Outlook: The company is focused on long-term investments in plant manufacturing operations to meet new consumer demands, improve product quality, expand capacity, and increase operational efficiencies.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Plant Expansion: The company is pursuing a plant expansion to meet higher demand for certain products, with plans to invest approximately $100 million over the next seven years. This expansion includes new machinery, equipment, and infrastructure improvements aimed at increasing operational efficiencies and product quality.
  • Capital Management: In 2024, the company repurchased and retired $13.5 million of its outstanding shares and paid cash dividends totaling $25.5 million. The company maintains a conservative financial position with no special financing arrangements or off-balance sheet entities. Cash flows from operations and short-term investment maturities are expected to meet financing needs, including capital expenditures. The company also manages a VEBA trust to fund future employee benefits, with no contributions made in 2024.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates higher cocoa and chocolate costs in 2025 due to expiring supply contracts, which may impact margins. It plans to continue monitoring input costs and may consider price adjustments to maintain profitability. Additionally, the company is open to potential acquisitions, which could involve bank borrowings or other financing. The plant expansion is expected to be funded from internal sources, with most expenditures occurring in 2025 and 2026.

Challenges and Risks

  • Supply Chain Disruptions: Potential disruptions due to natural disasters, pandemics, or geopolitical events could impact the availability and cost of raw materials.
  • Regulatory Risks: Changes in governmental laws and regulations, particularly those affecting food advertising, ingredient use, and international trade, could adversely impact operations.
  • Environmental and Climate Change Risks: Increased focus on climate change and environmental regulations could lead to higher costs and operational challenges.
  • Cybersecurity Risks: Reliance on computer systems exposes the company to risks of cyber-attacks and data breaches.
  • Labor Risks: Labor stoppages or shortages could disrupt production and distribution.
  • Pension Plan Risks: Participation in a multi-employer pension plan that is in 'critical and declining status' poses financial risks.
  • Management’s Discussion and Analysis: The company experienced a decrease in net product sales in 2024 due to lower sales volumes and resistance to price increases. Management is focused on restoring margins through price adjustments and cost reductions. The company is also investing in plant expansions to meet demand and improve operational efficiencies. The Spanish subsidiary continues to incur losses, and management is exploring strategies to improve its performance.
  • Market Risk Disclosures: The company is exposed to market risks related to commodity prices, interest rates, and foreign exchange. It uses commodity futures and foreign currency forward contracts to hedge against these risks. The company faces potential changes in input costs due to volatility in commodity markets and geopolitical factors. Interest rate risks are managed through a 'ladder' approach to investments, and foreign currency risks are mitigated through hedging strategies.

data.title: GLOBAL PARTNERS LP SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Global Partners LP, a leading independent owner, supplier, and operator of gasoline stations and convenience stores in the Northeast United States, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges and risks it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Total Sales: $17,163.6 million, reflecting a 4% increase primarily due to an increase in volume sold, partially offset by a decrease in prices.

Gross Profit: $1,057.9 million, an increase of 9% from the previous year, driven by favorable market conditions in gasoline blendstocks and distillates.

Net Income: $110.3 million, a decrease from the previous year, impacted by higher interest expenses and less favorable market conditions in certain segments.

EBITDA: $389.4 million, showing an improvement due to acquisitions and favorable market conditions.

Adjusted EBITDA: $388.9 million, reflecting adjustments for asset sales and joint venture activities.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company operates under three segments: Wholesale, Gasoline Distribution and Station Operations (GDSO), and Commercial. The Wholesale segment involves logistics of selling, gathering, blending, storing, and transporting refined petroleum products, gasoline blendstocks, renewable fuels, crude oil, and propane. The GDSO segment includes sales of branded and unbranded gasoline to gasoline station operators and sub-jobbers, as well as convenience store and prepared food sales. The Commercial segment includes sales and deliveries to end-user customers in the public sector and to large commercial and industrial end users of unbranded gasoline, home heating oil, diesel, kerosene, residual oil, and bunker fuel.

Geographical Performance: The company has a significant presence in the Northeast United States, with operations spanning from Maine to Florida and into the U.S. Gulf States. It is one of the largest independent owners, suppliers, and operators of gasoline stations and convenience stores in the Northeast, Maryland, and Virginia. The company also operates 64 gasoline stations in Texas through its joint venture, SPR.

Sales Units: The company sold approximately 6.6 billion gallons of refined petroleum products, gasoline blendstocks, renewable fuels, and crude oil in 2024, an increase from 5.7 billion gallons in 2023. This includes increases of 916 million gallons in the Wholesale segment and 48 million gallons in the Commercial segment, offset by a decrease of 44 million gallons in the GDSO segment.

New Production Launches: In 2024, the company acquired four refined-product terminals from Gulf Oil and one liquid energy terminal from ExxonMobil, expanding its terminal network. These acquisitions were financed through borrowings under the company's revolving credit facility.

Future Outlook: The company anticipates maintenance capital expenditures of approximately $60.0 million to $70.0 million and expansion capital expenditures, excluding acquisitions, of approximately $75.0 million to $85.0 million in 2025, focusing on investments in its gasoline station and terminal businesses. The company also plans to continue pursuing acquisitions and growth projects to expand its operations.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: In 2024, the company undertook several strategic initiatives, including the acquisition of four refined-product terminals from Gulf Oil and one liquid energy terminal from ExxonMobil, with a combined acquisition price of approximately $215.1 million. These acquisitions were financed through borrowings under the company's revolving credit facility. Additionally, the company issued $450 million of 8.250% senior notes due 2032 to repay a portion of borrowings under its credit agreement and for general corporate purposes.

Capital Management: The company reallocated $300 million of its revolving credit facility to the working capital revolving credit facility and reduced the accordion feature from $200 million to $0, resulting in a total commitment of $1.55 billion. It also redeemed all outstanding Series A Preferred Units for $70.4 million. The company paid cash distributions to common and preferred unitholders, totaling $121.6 million, and repurchased common units worth $14.2 million under its repurchase program. The company maintained a working capital of $207.2 million at the end of 2024.

Future Outlook: The company expects maintenance capital expenditures of approximately $60.0 million to $70.0 million and expansion capital expenditures of approximately $75.0 million to $85.0 million in 2025, focusing on investments in gasoline station and terminal businesses. The company plans to continue leveraging its credit facilities and explore opportunities for further acquisitions and growth projects to enhance its operational capacity and market presence.

Challenges and Risks

Market Risks: The company is exposed to significant market risks, including price volatility in refined petroleum products, gasoline blendstocks, renewable fuels, and crude oil. This volatility can impact margins and cash flow, especially in backwardation markets where future prices are lower than current prices.

Operational Risks: The company relies heavily on marine, pipeline, rail, and truck transportation services. Disruptions in these services due to severe weather, accidents, or labor disputes could adversely affect logistics activities. Additionally, the company is exposed to trade credit risk and performance risk in its supply chain, which could lead to financial losses if customers or suppliers fail to perform.

Regulatory Risks: The company is subject to extensive federal, state, and municipal regulations, including environmental laws that could increase operational costs. Changes in government mandates and tax credits could affect the availability and pricing of ethanol and renewable fuels, impacting sales. The enactment of derivatives legislation could also affect the company's ability to use derivative instruments for risk management.

Emerging Risks: Climate change poses a series of risks, including potential regulatory changes and physical impacts from severe weather events. The company may face increased costs and reduced demand for fossil fuels as a result of climate change initiatives and consumer shifts towards alternative energy sources.

Cybersecurity Risks: The company relies on operational and information technology systems, which are vulnerable to cyberattacks and other disruptions. A significant breach could compromise sensitive data and disrupt operations, leading to financial and reputational damage.

Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the challenges posed by market volatility and regulatory changes. The company is focused on maintaining flexibility in its operations and financial strategies to mitigate these risks. This includes managing inventory levels, optimizing logistics, and exploring opportunities for growth through acquisitions and joint ventures.

Interest Rate Risks: The company is exposed to interest rate risks due to variable rates on its credit agreements. A significant increase in interest rates could affect the company's ability to service its debt. Additionally, fluctuations in foreign currency exchange rates could impact financial results, although the company primarily operates in the U.S. market.

data.title: FIVE STAR BANCORP SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Five Star Bancorp, headquartered in the greater Sacramento metropolitan area, has released its 2024 10-K report, detailing the company's financial and operational performance over the past year. Operating through its wholly owned subsidiary, Five Star Bank, the company provides a broad range of banking products and services to small and medium-sized businesses, professionals, and individuals primarily in Northern California. The report highlights significant financial metrics, business developments, strategic initiatives, and the challenges and risks faced by the company.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Interest Income: $119.7 million, increased by $8.8 million or 7.96% compared to the previous year, primarily due to higher average balances and yields on loans.
  • Net Income: $45.7 million, a decrease from $47.7 million in the previous year, impacted by increased provision for credit losses and non-interest expenses.
  • Earnings per Common Share - Basic: $2.26, decreased from $2.78 in the previous year, reflecting the decline in net income.
  • Earnings per Common Share - Diluted: $2.26, consistent with basic EPS, decreased from $2.78 in the previous year.
  • Provision for Credit Losses: $6.95 million, increased by $2.95 million or 73.75% from the previous year, driven by loan growth and changes in credit quality.

Business Highlights

  • Company Overview: Five Star Bancorp operates through its wholly owned subsidiary, Five Star Bank, providing a broad range of banking products and services to small and medium-sized businesses, professionals, and individuals primarily in Northern California through eight branch offices.
  • Geographical Expansion: In 2024, Five Star Bank expanded its operations into the San Francisco Bay Area, including the opening of a full-service branch in Downtown San Francisco in September 2024.
  • Lending Activities: The company focuses primarily on commercial lending, with an emphasis on commercial real estate. As of December 31, 2024, commercial real estate loans represented 80.75% of total loans before deferred fees.
  • Product and Service Offerings: Five Star Bank offers a variety of loans including commercial real estate, commercial land and construction, residential real estate, farmland, and SBA loans. The bank also provides a complete array of deposit products and services.
  • New Branch Launch: A new full-service branch was opened in Downtown San Francisco in September 2024, marking a significant expansion into the San Francisco Bay Area.
  • Human Capital: As of December 31, 2024, the company employed 205 full-time and five part-time employees, emphasizing a commitment to an inclusive, safe, and healthy workplace.
  • Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Initiatives: The company is executing an ESG strategy focusing on environmental impacts, social factors, and governance, including efforts to reduce its carbon footprint and support community development.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates that interest rates may be lowered over the next few years, which could have a beneficial impact on net interest income due to its liability sensitivity.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: Five Star Bancorp aims to grow organically by leveraging its core competencies, focusing on expanding its lending growth, and enhancing its technology platforms to improve efficiency. The company is also committed to engaging in the economic development of its communities and market areas.
  • Capital Management: In 2024, Five Star Bancorp raised approximately $80.9 million through a public offering of common stock to support growth and working capital needs. The company maintains a strong liquidity position with cash and cash equivalents representing 9.90% of total deposits. It also has access to significant borrowing capacity through the FHLB, Federal Reserve Discount Window, and correspondent bank lines of credit. The company paid $16.2 million in dividends during 2024 and plans to continue its practice of paying quarterly cash dividends.
  • Future Outlook: Five Star Bancorp projects spending $0.7 million on IT systems and processes and allocating $17.1 million for dividends in 2025. The company plans to maintain its focus on organic growth, leveraging its existing strengths, and managing its liquidity to support ongoing operations and strategic initiatives. The company also anticipates continued monitoring of its loan to deposit ratio to ensure regulatory compliance and operational efficiency.

Challenges and Risks

  • Concentration Risks: The company faces significant risks due to its concentration in Northern California, making it vulnerable to local economic downturns.
  • Competitive Landscape: The competitive landscape is intensifying with both traditional and new financial service providers, which could pressure margins and market share.
  • Regulatory Compliance: Regulatory compliance remains a critical challenge, with stringent capital requirements and consumer protection laws posing potential operational constraints.
  • Cybersecurity Threats: Cybersecurity threats and data privacy issues are also prominent risks, with potential breaches impacting reputation and financial stability.
  • Real Estate Market Dependency: The company is heavily reliant on the real estate market, particularly in Northern California, which exposes it to fluctuations in property values and associated credit risks.
  • Interest Rate Volatility: Interest rate volatility is another major concern, as it affects net interest income and could lead to increased loan defaults and reduced demand for loans.
  • Technological Changes: The rapid technological changes in the financial services industry require significant investment to remain competitive, posing a risk if the company fails to keep pace.
  • Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the heightened competition and regulatory pressures as key challenges. Strategies to address these include enhancing technological capabilities and expanding geographically to mitigate regional economic risks. The company is also focusing on diversifying its loan portfolio to reduce dependency on the real estate sector.
  • Market Risk: The company is exposed to interest rate risk, which could adversely affect profitability. The Federal Reserve's monetary policies, including interest rate adjustments, significantly impact the company's financial performance. Management is implementing asset-liability management strategies to mitigate these risks, although the effectiveness of these strategies is uncertain given the current economic volatility.

data.title: Descartes Systems Fiscal Q4 Results Expected to Beat Consensus on Organic Growth, Acquisitions, RBC Says
data.description:
Descartes Systems is expected to report fiscal Q4 revenue and earnings ahead of consensus estimates, supported by stable organic growth and increased contributions from acquisitions, RBC Capital Markets said in an earnings preview on Friday.

The brokerage forecasts Q4 revenue of $173 million, up 17% year-over-year, slightly above consensus, and GAAP earnings per share of $0.46, exceeding consensus of $0.43.

Adjusted Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization is projected at $77 million, also up 17% year over year, surpassing Descartes' long-term target of 10%-15% annual growth. Organic growth is expected to hold steady at 7%, with acquisitions such as Sellercloud and MyCarrierPortal adding $15 million in revenue, according to the note.

RBC noted that uncertainty surrounding US tariffs may have pulled forward trade volumes and increased demand for Descartes' tariff-related services, providing a potential tailwind. The freight market showed signs of improvement in Q4, with record US import volumes in January and strong air cargo activity, though trucking remained weak.

Looking ahead, the brokerage expects the company's Q1 baseline guidance to slightly exceed consensus, with historical trends implying Q1 actuals of $176 million in revenue and $79 million in adjusted EBITDA, above the Street's forecast of $172 million and $76 million, respectively.

The brokerage reiterates its outperform rating on Descartes' stock, with a price target of $133.

data.title: Investors unnerved by heated Trump-Zelensky Oval Office showdown
data.description:
Investors were stunned on Friday after Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy's meeting with U.S. President Donald Trump ended in disaster, adding uncertainty to financial markets already jittery due to weakening economic data and volatility around U.S. trade policies.

The two leaders traded verbal blows before the world's media at the White House, pushing markets to react with a risk-off bid for safe-haven Treasuries as the public spat added uncertainty over the prospect of a peace deal with Russia.

"It’s disturbing," said Jack McIntyre, portfolio manager at Brandywine Global. "It looked like we were moving towards progress on a peace deal or a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine and maybe now that gets to come on hold, so you have to price in a little bit more uncertainty," he said.

Zelenskiy's U.S. visit was aimed to keep the U.S. from aligning with Russian President Vladimir Putin, who launched the Ukraine invasion three years ago. Instead, he clashed with Trump and Vice President JD Vance over the war, highlighting Kyiv’s struggle to retain U.S. backing. Trump later accused Zelenskiy of disrespecting the United States.

Benchmark 10-year Treasury yields, which move inversely to prices, declined after the public confrontation and were last seen at 4.229% from about 4.27% earlier on Friday.

European stock futures fell while U.S. stock indexes lost ground on Friday. The Dax and CAC40 futures (FCEc1) fell 0.6% and the Eurostoxx 50 futures dropped as much as 1.4% and were last down 0.8%. The S&P 500 index was last down 0.02%. The euro fell by as much as 0.37% to a two-week low of $1.036, before paring some of that decline to trade at $1.0366.

data.title: Advance Auto Parts' Path Forward Depends on Execution of Transformation Plan, UBS Says
data.description:
Advance Auto Parts' future direction hinges on the implementation of its transformation strategy and achieving revenue growth, UBS said in a note Friday.

The investment firm expects the stock to be rangebound as the market awaits evidence of progress in the execution of the company's plan to transform itself following its Q4 results and latest update, which were perceived to be underwhelming.

"The retailer will need to simultaneously implement various new merchandising strategies, operating enhancements and supply chain developments," UBS said.

While bullish investors think the company is making "solid progress" on several persistent issues, the firm said the bearish ones are holding their ground and convinced that the company "can't avoid inevitable new issues coming in the future and overshadowing progress made elsewhere."

UBS trimmed its price target to $42 from $44, while maintaining a neutral rating on the stock.

data.title: CBOT corn slumps on tariff anxiety, year-end positioning
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade corn futures eased on Friday as traders worried about the impact of possible new U.S. tariffs on goods from Mexico, Canada and China.

  • CBOT May corn (CK25) settled down 11-1/2 cents at $4.69-1/2 a bushel after falling to its lowest point since January 10.

  • Market players feared retaliation against U.S. agricultural exports if tariffs are implemented on March 4, as U.S. President Donald Trump said they would be in a social media post on Thursday.

data.description: U.S. natural gas futures end the week down 7.1% as warmer weather forecasts for early March are seen slowing the pace of inventory withdrawals in the final stretch of winter.
